articles.title: 14 Effective SEO Tips for Travel Websites
articles.fullContent:
Key Takeaways

  • Travel buyers move through a long, multi-stage journey (dreaming, research, planning, booking) and search for something different at each step, so your content needs to meet them at all four. 
  • Nearly 40 percent of U.S. travelers used generative AI tools to plan trips in 2025, which means visibility in ChatGPT, Google’s AI Overviews, and similar tools is now as important as ranking in traditional SERPs. 
  • Long-tail, location-specific, and experience-based keywords drive more bookings than broad terms, since travelers increasingly search for specific events, neighborhoods, and itineraries rather than generic destinations. 
  • Schema markup, fresh reviews, and backlinks from trusted travel publications are the clearest signals that both Google and AI tools use to decide whether to cite your brand. 
  • Travel SEO compounds over time, with a single well-built destination guide capable of driving bookings for years, which makes organic one of the highest-ROI channels in travel marketing.

Are you looking to increase traffic to your travel website? Then, you might as well get a piece of the pie.

The travel industry is lucrative, and the competition is fierce. Collectively, U.S. travel alone accounts for $2.9 trillion in economic activity each year. That indicates there are many active players, but also ample opportunities for those who can differentiate themselves. Businesses in this space need an effective travel SEO strategy to remain competitive and drive more customers.

Travel SEO helps websites become more visible in search engine results pages (SERPs) when implemented effectively. In addition, there are multiple strategies you can introduce to bolster the SEO for your travel website.

In this article, I’ll discuss proven strategies to improve SEO for travel websites, making them more competitive in the SERPs.

SEO Tips for The Travel Industry

Plenty of travel SEO overlaps with what works in any other industry. On-page tactics, such as keyword research and link building, and technical strategies like image optimization, all matter regardless of what you sell.

That being said, travel users do behave differently from other audiences. The way they research and purchase changes the playing field for travel SEO keywords. Travelers tend to start with broad, dreamy searches (“best beach vacations in November”) and only narrow down to high-intent terms (“nonstop flights JFK to Lisbon”) much later. That creates a sprawling map of potential keywords, many of which are weirdly specific. There are also subcategories like luxury travel SEO with keywords of their own.

This unique keyword strategy is only further impacted by AI. According to Phocuswright, nearly 40 percent of U.S. travelers used generative AI tools to plan trips in 2025, an 11-point jump in just one year. Those early-funnel, “dreamy” queries users are asking are now being entered into platforms like ChatGPT and Claude, rather than traditional search engines. Your answers to the broad range of early-stage travel keywords must now be answered and structured in a way that gets AI’s attention.

Additionally, travel SEO services consultant Dan Taylor identified an interesting trend in his 2025 SEO outlook. Customers are also moving toward experience-based travel and focusing less on general destinations. Instead of taking “A trip to Arizona to see the mountains,” users are traveling for specific events. Spending on experiences has increased 65 percent overall in recent years, with events like Taylor Swift concerts and FIFA World Cup matches causing meaningful jumps in bookings and demand.

Dan says, “Users are looking to leverage subject matter experts in helping them get the most out of their holidays, and compare experiences and not just hotel stars and prices.”That has real implications for how you build a travel site. You need content that ranks for thousands of long-tail itinerary and destination-related queries, not just your booking pages. You need authority signals that Google can trust in the SERPs and AI Overviews. And you need patience, because the journey from first search to confirmed booking is rarely a straight line.

Why Travel SEO is Essential

Source: https://www.adido-digital.co.uk/insights/the-role-of-seo-in-travel-marketing/

A strong SEO strategy gives travel businesses something paid ads can’t: a presence at every stage of a trip-planning journey that can last weeks or months. Travelers don’t go from “I want a vacation” to “book now” in one search. They cycle through four distinct stages, and they Google something different at each step.

Here’s what the buyer journey looks like for travel brands specifically:

  • Dreaming stage: Someone searches “best places to visit in spring.” If your destination guide ranks, you’re shaping their wish list before they’ve even picked a country.
  • Research stage: They start narrowing in with queries like “Is Lisbon safe for solo travelers?” or “Costa Rica vs. Belize.” This is where comparison pages and expert guides build trust.
  • Planning stage: Searches get specific. “Best boutique hotels in Lisbon” or “3-day Lisbon itinerary.” Your landing pages and reviews do most of the heavy lifting here.
  • Booking stage: High-intent terms like “Lisbon hotel near Alfama” or “nonstop flights to Lisbon” are where the conversion happens. If your pages are optimized and your site loads fast, this is your moment.

Beyond reach, SEO traffic actually converts better than other digital channels in travel. And unlike paid campaigns, the work compounds. A guide you publish today can keep pulling in bookings two years from now, which makes organic one of the few channels with real long-term ROI for travel brands.

articles.title: History of social media: Timeline (1973-2026)
articles.fullContent:
Key takeaways

  1. Social media’s roots stretch back to the 1970s, but the platforms we recognize today began emerging in the late 1990s.
  2. The 2004 to 2012 period saw explosive growth as Facebook, YouTube, Twitter, and Instagram each redefined how people connect and share content online.
  3. Short-form video, the creator economy, and algorithm-driven feeds have dominated the 2016 to 2026 era.
  4. Social media now reaches more than 5 billion users globally, and the next chapter is being shaped by regulation, decentralization, and AI integration.

What is social media?

Social media refers to online platforms that let people create profiles, connect with others, and share user-generated content in real time. It is defined less by any single app and more by a set of shared traits that separate a social network from a website or a chat tool.

That distinction matters when you try to name the “first” platform, because the answer depends on where you draw the line. Most social media platforms share a few core characteristics:

  • User-created profiles: A personal identity or account that represents you on the platform.
  • Connections with others: The ability to follow, friend, or link to other users.
  • Content sharing: Tools to post text, photos, video, or links to an audience.
  • Two-way interaction: Comments, likes, replies, and messages that make communication interactive rather than one-directional.

With that lens in place, the rest of this timeline becomes easier to read as a steady evolution rather than a single invention.

Bonus !!!

Download a free 18-minute social media plan template to keep your daily social media task list always at hand and start growing your followers fast.

Download now

What are the key milestones in the social media timeline from 1973 to 2026?

Here is a scannable social media timeline covering the most significant milestones in the history of social media, from the earliest online communities to today.

What came before social media? Early online communities (1973-1996)

Long before profiles and feeds, people were already connecting through networked computers. These proto-social platforms answer the question of when did social media start, and they laid the groundwork for everything that followed.

The PLATO system, developed by the University of Illinois in 1970, had many features of an early social media network but was largely used in academia. Around the same time, Community Memory (1973) set up one of the first public computerized bulletin boards, letting people post and read messages at shared terminals.

These systems were text-only and technical, but they proved something important: people wanted to use computers to talk to each other, not just to run calculations. That desire is the thread that connects a 1970s bulletin board to a modern feed.

How did bulletin boards evolve into buddy lists?

The building blocks of modern social media came together across the 1980s and early 1990s. Tom Truscott and Jim Ellis created Usenet in 1979-1980, a distributed discussion system that is often cited as the first open social media application, which helps answer who created social media.

Internet Relay Chat (IRC) arrived in 1988 and popularized real-time group conversation. GeoCities launched in 1994 and gave people a way to build personal web pages, and AOL Instant Messenger soon made buddy lists and status messages part of everyday online life.

Together, these tools taught a generation the habits, from messaging friends to sharing interests in public, that modern social media platforms would later scale to billions.

What was the first wave of social networks (1997-2005)?

The late 1990s and early 2000s brought the first social media platform most people would recognize, along with a rush of profile-based networks. This era is where the modern model, a personal profile plus a list of connections, truly took shape.

articles.title: Every 2026 World Cup Ad Ranked
articles.fullContent:
Every World Cup Ad Ranked by EDO’s TV Outcomes

The 2026 World Cup has come to an end, and now it’s time to find out which ads scored big.

TV data and measurement company EDO has calculated the effectiveness of every ad during the World Cup and has shared the results with ADWEEK. The ads are ranked by EDO’s TV Outcomes Score, which measures the impact of every ad during the entire FIFA World Cup tournament (June 11-July 19, 2026)—across English and Spanish language broadcasts—based on the immediate consumer engagements most predictive of future sales. Ads on this list are ranked by TV Outcomes, which calculates how much more engagement they drove than the average ad during the World Cup.

For example, a score of 1,000 means the ad drove 10 times (1,000/100) as much consumer engagement compared to the average ad during the World Cup. By measuring these proven, investment-grade ad engagement signals at a granular level, EDO’s TV Outcomes Score shows which World Cup ads were most effective at driving consumers closer to purchase—based on outcomes, not opinions. You can find even more detailed World Cup ad performance data at edo.com/worldcup . (Photo: Koji Watanabe/Getty Images)

articles.title: Women’s Sports Are Rewriting the Fandom Playbook
articles.fullContent:
By Ashley Cisneros Mejia The future of brands gets decided here. Join the industry’s top marketers at Brandweek for the ideas, insights, and connections shaping what’s next. Get your ticket.

This post was created in partnership with Scripps Sports

Where does fandom begin now? Rarely at tipoff.

During an ADWEEK House Sports Summit fireside chat co-hosted with Scripps Sports, Sherry Pitkofsky, SVP of marketing at Scripps Sports, sat down with Alexis Ohanian, tech entrepreneur and founder of Seven Seven Six, and Dawn Harper-Nelson, Olympic gold and silver medalist and Emmy Award-winning broadcaster, to discuss how belonging shapes the next phase of sports fandom.

Scripps Sports built its portfolio for fans who follow athletes well past game day. And Scripps’ ION network takes that commitment to women’s sports. The network includes dedicated WNBA and NWSL nights, ATHLOS track and field, professional women’s hockey, Major League Volleyball, and regional teams across the country. The 24/7 network gives fans live sports, original storytelling, and, importantly, connection, she said.

“It’s more than what’s just on the field or on the court or on the pitch,” Pitkofsky noted. Fans want the athletes, teams, social conversation, and stories behind the score.

Emerging leagues can’t wait for legacy media economics to catch up to the needs of modern fans, Ohanian argued. Women’s sports are busy proving there’s a faster way.

“If we think about the way fandom is built now, the way any of these emerging leagues, men’s or women’s, but especially women’s, have to build their fan base, it’s brick by brick,” he explained. “It’s community-driven. It’s bottom up. It’s mostly online to offline.”

Ohanian credited partners like Scripps Sports for getting access right. A new league can fight for an exclusive rights deal, he cautioned, but the early money is too small to satisfy anyone. It’s smarter to maximize reach, win fans online daily, and make watching effortless once people fall in love with the athletes. For marketers, the payoff is fans who behave like insiders instead of spectators.

The industry spent years packaging women’s sports as inspiration. Harper-Nelson wants that framing retired. Female athletes are complete competitors, edge included.

“I know that yes, I am a female athlete, but when I step on that line, I’m trying to kill anything in sight,” Harper-Nelson said. “My dream will live, and yours will die. I’m an athlete. I am here to handle this mission. I have sacrificed my life for this. Stop holding me to your standard.”

Medals draw a crowd. Candor keeps one. Harper-Nelson urged storytellers to look past the victory lap.

Pitkofsky raised another shift: Athletes now reach fans directly, no intermediary required. Harper-Nelson has felt the difference firsthand.

“You can truly create a community that can feel like family on social media,” Harper-Nelson said.

Ownership can turn athletes into builders

Ohanian thinks ATHLOS could be a model for women’s sports. The all-women’s professional track and field league Ohanian founded pays real prize money and hands athletes equity that grows with its success.

“I want our best athletes to act like owners and feel like owners and be owners,” he said.

And in many ways, these athletes are more important than owners, like Ohanian.

“No one is showing up at ATHLOS to watch me. It is the athletes first and foremost who drive outsized value to these organizations,” he said. “I think that somehow it got lost traditionally in sports, and we have a chance to get it right now with women.”

articles.title: Week of July 6 Cable News Ratings: MS NOW Grows in Demo and Viewers
articles.fullContent:
By Mark Mwachiro This is TVNewser’s basic cable network ranker and cable news report for the week of July 6, 2026.

MS NOW finished the first full week of July as the only network with gains in total viewers and the Adults 25-54 demo during primetime and total day. Making it even more impressive was that the network was up by double digits in both measured categories during primetime.

Meanwhile, Fox News and CNN came down from their Independence Day celebration coverage highs to finish the week down across the board. However, Fox News was still the most-watched cable news network.

This week also featured the cable news networks covering the shocking news of South Carolina Senator Lindsey Graham ’s passing early Sunday morning. MS NOW was the first to break the news at 2:49 a.m. ET, followed by Fox News at 2:52 a.m. ET,  and not too far behind at CNN at 2:53 a.m. ET.

According to Nielsen big data + panel for the week of July 6, Fox News averaged 2.109 million total viewers and 164,000 A25-54 viewers during primetime. The network was down -15% in total viewers and -25% in the demo compared to the week prior ( the week beginning June 29 ).

During total day viewing, Fox News averaged 1.429 million total viewers and 127,000 A25-54 viewers. Based on its average from the previous week’s measurements, the network was down -12% in total viewers and -17% in the demo.

Fox News remained in first place in total primetime viewership and slipped to third in demo viewership among all basic cable networks. During total day, it continued to be No. 1 in total viewers and was second in the total day demo.

MS NOW averaged 1.028 million total viewers and 94,000 viewers in the A25-54 demo for the week. The network grew by +11% in total viewers and +19% in the demo compared to its primetime average the week prior.

During total day, MS NOW averaged 606,000 total viewers and 59,000 demo viewers. It was up +2% in total viewers and +4% in the demo compared to the network’s average the week prior—the only network to gain viewers in both categories across the two measured dayparts.

Among all basic cable networks, MS NOW finished in second place in total primetime viewers and was 15th in the demo. It was No. 2 in total viewers and in ninth place in the total day demo.

CNN averaged 582,00 total primetime viewers and 95,000 A25-54 viewers during primetime for the week of July 6. The network saw declines of -13% in total viewers and -8% in the demo compared to the previous week.

During total day, the network had 442,000 total viewers and 58,000 viewers in the demo. CNN was down by -4% in total viewers and -3% in the demo during this daypart.

Among all cable networks, CNN dropped from third to fifth in total viewers and was 14th in the demo during primetime. It remained in fourth place in total day among total viewers and was 10th in the demo.

When looking at these networks’ performance during the same period a year ago :

During primetime, Fox News was down -13% in total viewers and -37% in the A25-54 demo. During total day, the network posted losses of -7% in total viewers and -33% in the demo.

MS NOW was up +16% in total viewers and +9% in the demo. During total day, the network grew by +14% in total viewers and +13% in the demo.

In primetime, CNN saw gains of +20% in total viewers and +7% in the demo. During total day, it grew by +20% in total viewers but was down -5% in the demo.

Fox News had 13 out of the 15 spots of the most-watched cable news shows of the week, with The Five on top with 3.331 million total viewers at 5 p.m. ET. MS NOW was represented in the chart by the Monday airing of The Rachel Maddow Show at 9 p.m. ET, with 1.829 million total viewers at No. 8, and by The Last Word with Lawrence O’Donnell , airing weeknights at 10 p.m. ET, with 1.396 million total viewers at No. 15.

The Five also finished first in the Adults 25-54 demo, averaging 279,000 viewers at 5 p.m. ET. Fox News held 13 of the 15 top spots in the demo, with The Rachel Maddow Show in 14th place with 141,000 viewers. The Last Word with Lawrence O’Donnell finished with 134,000 viewers at No. 15.

articles.title: 5 Lessons From the World Cup’s Best Marketing Plays—and Those That Missed the Mark
articles.fullContent:
By Pio Schunker The future of brands gets decided here. Join the industry’s top marketers at Brandweek for the ideas, insights, and connections shaping what’s next. Get your ticket.

The 2026 FIFA World Cup captured more than $10 billion in global ad spend, according to WARC Media estimates. That’s on top of the $2.8 billion FIFA collected from its 20 official sponsors, and before counting the dozens of non-sponsor brands that piled on for free.

That’s an extraordinary amount of noise fighting for the same six billion potential viewers in the same six weeks.

And that’s the main question the C-suite at these advertisers will be asking of their CMOs: Did their marketing break through and, ultimately, drive commercial success?

Here are five lessons they can learn from the brands who did it right—and a few who missed opportunities along the way.

1. Build for the long-tail, not just the peak

Most World Cup advertisers planned a six week run and stopped there. Adidas did it differently. Starting with its five minute film starring Timothée Chalamet, the campaign was built on a long marketing runway and attached to real commercial goals.

“Backyard Legends” is the latest chapter of “You Got This,” a campaign that’s been running since the 2024 Paris Olympics. It was preceded by successful product drops from Bad Bunny and Messi in early 2026, and followed by nationwide pop-ups inside Nordstrom stores, a Dick’s retail push timed to Messi’s Algeria hat-trick, and fan experiences across five host cities through its app.

Timothée Chalamet Builds His Dream Soccer Team in Adidas’ World Cup Short The widespread effort paid off. On its April 2026 earnings call, Adidas said it booked roughly $292 million in World Cup product sales before kickoff, driving revenue up 7% to €6.6 billion. CEO Bjørn Gulden tied the campaign to Adidas closing the U.S. gap with Nike and guiding its full year to high-single-digit growth.

In that way, the World Cup became fuel for Adidas’ whole year, not a standalone spike.

Lesson No. 1: Start the drip months before your next tentpole and integrate retail, product, app, and content from the start to keep the campaign working well after the event is done.

Many brands leaned into similar messages of communal joy, tension, the sheer love of “footie.” But hiding in plain sight, the tournament was telling a sharper story that no brand tapped into: the underdog against Goliath, the comeback from behind, the will to win that defines a champion.

Cabo Verde, a nation of 500,000 ranked 67th, held Spain scoreless and took Messi’s Argentina to extra time before falling 3-2; Spain and Argentina overcame all odds to win against France and England, respectively; and Spain ultimately beat out defending champs Argentina for the World Cup Championship title. This is what the internet talked about.

Brands like Nike missed the opportunity to tap into that conversation. Its “Rip the Script” platform was expansive enough to embrace all the content it developed, but failed to celebrate what the world was discussing as it happened. That dynamism would have given Nike’s campaign even greater relevance.

Lesson No. 2: Find the story not being told. Create a platform that has the flexibility to pivot from pre-produced content to on-the-field surprises so you can stay relevant throughout the tournament and after.

How Lionel Messi Became the Face of 1 in 4 World Cup Ads 3.  Audit your talent

Another familiar playbook this year was using celebrities to make the game accessible to a new audience, honoring both the familiar legends and the new guard.

That’s exactly where things went wrong. Suddenly, everyone was casting the same eight celebs. Beckham appeared in ads for more than ten sponsors. Messi showed up in almost 25% of tournament spots. It became hard to know which brand featured which player.

YouGov found roughly 40% of U.S. World Cup followers noticed sponsors, but noticing and attributing are different. Shared casting erodes brand recognition massively.

Lesson No. 3: Brands avoid getting lost in a sea of celeb-sameness by ensuring their category conflict clauses extend to adjacent categories and by standing up social listening tools that track share of voice by talent.

The best work made the product and brand inseparable from the event itself.

Brahma Beer’s World Cup ad exuded the sheer joy of Brazil, as well as its cultural pride in football and the attendant skepticism about it, while keeping the product central to the storyline. Nike, conversely, could have been any brand’s sports marketing.

Lesson No. 4: Could you swap your product for a rival’s without changing the ad? If so, that’s a prop, not a winning proposition.

6 Top Local World Cup Ads: Adidas Backs Scotland, Brahma Dreams Big For Brazil and More 5. Don’t broadcas t, have a conversation

So many brands missed an opportunity to create the two-way dialogue crucial to reaching Gen Z and Millennials.

Packaging—the most underrated medium in brand-building—had a great highlight in Café Bustelo’s “Game Face” campaign. The brand shipped more than one million cans, designed by hyper-local artists across Mexico, Brazil, Argentina, and Colombia, with temporary face-tattoo kits. The tattoos went viral, as people used them to promote their flag, their country, their teams, themselves, and the product—in one fell swoop.

Lesson No. 5: When trying to reach younger audiences, don’t broadcast your message; make it a conversation they can be a part of.

Pio Schunker is co-founder of strategic growth consultancy The Actionists and has held senior global marketing leadership roles at Samsung and The Coca-Cola Company.

articles.title: Social Marketing That Moves at the Speed of Sports
articles.fullContent:
By Justin Chin The future of brands gets decided here. Join the industry’s top marketers at Brandweek for the ideas, insights, and connections shaping what’s next. Get your ticket.

This post was created in partnership with Viral Nation

Dynamic action and big plays are what make live sports so compelling to audiences, but traditional brand partnerships that take time to plan and execute are too slow to keep up.

During an ADWEEK House Sports Summit panel co-hosted with Viral Nation, industry leaders sat down with Chelsea Bakken, head of audience development and social at ADWEEK, to discuss how brands can match the fast pace of sports by leveraging social media channels and working with creators.

Brandon Lentino, chief creative officer at Viral Nation, kicked off the conversation by talking about social media’s role during the recent FIFA World Cup. He specifically called out the channel’s power to amplify mom-and-pop businesses, such as the New Jersey deli that went viral for welcoming a British visitor with open arms.

“On a macro level, social media has been such fertile soil to share those stories,” Lentino said. “The only reason we’re hearing about that deli in Jersey is because there’s content getting created around it.”

Smaller businesses with a social media presence were able to capitalize on the tournament’s global appeal in real time with a “communal wave of user-generated content,” he added.

“When you take that already-existing groundswell of using social to get these local businesses out there, and then you mix in all of these different cultures experiencing them for the first time, it’s created this really beautiful showing of the world coming together in a way that I don’t think you’ve seen in previous FIFA tournaments,” Lentino explained.

Amber Balcaen, a professional racecar driver and entrepreneur, thinks of social media creators as broadcasters. And because of their emotional connection to creators, fans feel more like participants in sporting events than like passive viewers.

“Creators are able to give the fans that type of perspective that they might not be able to see by just turning it on TV,” Balcaen shared. “It makes that emotional attachment deeper and makes them be more invested in what they’re cheering for.”

Lentino agreed, mentioning IShowSpeed as a creator who became “as much a face of the World Cup as some of the actual athletes were.” Lentino attributes IShowSpeed’s success to remaining authentic in his own appreciation of the athletes. For brands, that combination of audience and authenticity is unmatched.

“You’re going to penetrate these communities more effectively with a voice that they actually want to hear from,” Lentino said. “And that’s not to say that traditional media can’t work or can’t be authentic, but it is a whole new wave of spending that needs to be considered.”

Balcaen wrapped up the panel with some advice for brands working with creators. Her headline was to allow the creator freedom to make it authentic rather than impose certain requirements on a piece of content.

“When brands are asking us creators to push their product within the first three seconds of a video, people are going to scroll right past that because they’ll know it’s an ad,” Balcaen explained. “If you truly want people to trust the creator that you’re working with, you need to be able to let them integrate your product or your brand in an authentic way.”

Justin Chin is a contributing writer for ADWEEK.

articles.title: The Key to Building More Durable Brand Relationships
articles.fullContent:
By Justin Chin The future of brands gets decided here. Join the industry’s top marketers at Brandweek for the ideas, insights, and connections shaping what’s next. Get your ticket.

This post was created in partnership with Strava

Professional sports offer brands compelling moments to reach audiences, but these are often one-off opportunities, like a championship game or an all-star weekend. How can brands move beyond such fleeting events to create authentic, lasting engagement?

During an ADWEEK House Sports Summit panel co-hosted with Strava, industry leaders sat down with Bill Bradley, deputy TV, media, and sports editor at ADWEEK, to discuss how brands should position themselves within the daily fitness routines of everyday people.

Jen Starr, director of North America at Strava for Business, opened the discussion by explaining that Strava’s goal is to help brands find ways to be part of a consumer’s daily fitness ritual organically.

“Instead of looking at it as a one-time campaign-based activation or impression-based activation, we look for an opportunity to consistently motivate their audience and to reward them for their effort,” Starr said.

As an example, she cited a recent collaboration with Starbucks that challenged Strava users to walk or run for 22 minutes a day for 10 days or more. Those who did were entered to win a limited-edition Starbucks weighted vest.

Jared Shulman, SVP, head of strategy at CAA Brand Consulting, noted that brands have to target the exercise moment as well as the routines before and after.

“It’s not just about running. “It’s how you get ready to run. It’s how you recover from running,” Shulman pointed out. “Look at Therabody—they are at run clubs and at fitness spaces because of the recovery. It’s this whole kind of ecosystem surrounding it that comes back to the routine and the rituals.”

Starr talked about the importance of run clubs within Strava’s ecosystem, which have become so popular that celebrities such as DJ and record producer Diplo have started their own and host events that are open to the public.

Their unique intersection of social connection and fitness, Starr noted, allows brands to show up to a targeted audience. One example she gave was a beauty brand that wanted a club to try a new line of SPF products by routing the runners to the store.

“There is opportunity for an authentic way in for so many brands who are really deeply understanding their audience and their customer and showing up where they are, which is at our run club,” Starr explained.

Baker Machado, host, producer, and moderator at Front Office Sports, shared how brands that partner with athletes and celebrities can outlast a moment by telling a story.

Machado mentioned NFL quarterback Fernando Mendoza’s partnership with LinkedIn as a “perfect example.” Throughout his NCAA championship season at Indiana University, Mendoza built a presence on the social networking platform that culminated on the night of the NFL Draft.

“In that period when he gets drafted by the Raiders at No. 1, he’s this LinkedIn spokesperson, and we’re all waiting for him to update his LinkedIn status to being selected as the No. 1 pick,” Machado said.

However, Machado cautioned that the story being told must be authentic, as Mendoza was with LinkedIn. “If the person who’s trying to sell me this product doesn’t love the product, I’m not going to love the product,” he concluded.

Justin Chin is a contributing writer for ADWEEK.

articles.title: How Snacking Brands Turn Sports Into Spending
articles.fullContent:
By Seth Simons The future of brands gets decided here. Join the industry’s top marketers at Brandweek for the ideas, insights, and connections shaping what’s next. Get your ticket.

This post was created in partnership with Ibotta

Live sports draw some of the largest audiences on the planet, and every watch party is a chance for snack brands to win the grocery basket.

During an ADWEEK House Sports Summit fireside chat co-hosted with Ibotta, Sara Wallace, head of brand marketing at Ibotta, sat down with Justin Parnell, SVP of marketing and insights at Mondelēz International, and Chris Dungan, VP of client partnerships at Ibotta, to discuss how brands connect cultural moments to real performance.

For a leading snacking company like Mondelēz, live sports are what Parnell described as a “critical moment of consumption.” They let brands be present with consumers and their families as they gather to enjoy their favorite sports and snacks. Stretching five weeks, the World Cup offered a long runway to sustain that relationship.

“We can have a continuous pulse of always being on with our consumer,” Parnell explained. “We partnered with celebrities. We had one-of-a-kind special experiences that fans and consumers were able to win, and we did special limited-edition packs and flavors all focused around the World Cup. That really helped us win in store.”

As consumers get more conscious about discretionary spending, brands find new ways to stand out.

“We spend a lot on displays and merchandising in store to ensure that we are getting that really valuable shelf space,” Parnell said. “And then where Ibotta comes in, we overlay specific coupons against the right audiences and incentives to ensure that we are closing that deal.”

From his bird’s-eye view at Ibotta, Dungan sees brands leaning in on promotions as a way to meet consumers where they are. “It’s not just with these cultural moments and sporting events, but all the time now, just making sure we’re putting the right offers to help deliver on consumer needs,” he observed. “We’re seeing a tremendous uptick in overall engagement with promotions right now.”

One example of that current engagement and promotions approach is SnackWorks Perks, a rewards program Mondelēz recently launched with Ibotta. Spanning Mondelēz brands from Oreo and Chips Ahoy to Ritz and Sour Patch Kids, the platform rewards consumers for buying across the portfolio. The goal is to drive frequency and loyalty over time.

In contrast to the vast Ibotta Performance Network, through which Mondelēz can reach hundreds of millions of consumers with strategic offers, SnackWorks Perks is a more bespoke platform for building personalized relationships with consumers who opt in.

“We’re learning from those consumers, and we’re building better relationships with them,” Dungan explained. “We’re seeing environmental things, like high-fiber trends and high-protein trends, and all of that data begins to show up in the understanding that we’re capturing in this network’s program.”

Such rich insights open up a new world of precision marketing. “Gone are the days of blanketing the world with one big message,” Parnell said. “We’re getting very specific: What is the consumer’s shopping behavior, what is their challenge, and how do we reach them in the most relevant way?”

Leveraging Ibotta’s transactional data, Mondelēz can build unique audiences—loyal buyers, for instance, or shoppers leaning toward competitors—and tailor offers accordingly.

“We can actually tailor our messages in a more direct and impactful way through the value of data,” Parnell concluded. “Ibotta is one of the partners that we are working closely with to unlock and unleash that capability.”

articles.title: How Sports and the Big Screen Deliver Unique Advertising Opportunities
articles.fullContent:
By Dawn Papandrea The future of brands gets decided here. Join the industry’s top marketers at Brandweek for the ideas, insights, and connections shaping what’s next. Get your ticket.

This post was created in partnership with Screenvision Media

Sports and cinema drive emotional experiences among communities of passionate viewers.

During an ADWEEK House Sports Summit panel co-hosted with Screenvision Media, Mark Stenberg, senior media reporter at ADWEEK, sat down with industry leaders to discuss why culturally relevant sports and moviegoing experiences are ideal settings for creating lasting brand impact.

Kevin McGaw, SVP and head of marketing at Screenvision Media, a cinema advertising organization, began the discussion by sharing the numerous parallels between cinema and sports fandom.

“There are people who are huge fans of sports who wear jerseys and paint their faces. Moviegoers will wear costumes and paint their faces,” McGaw shared. “That communal experience you only get from sports and moviegoing.”

Using World Cup watch parties as an example, McGaw pointed out that consumers enjoy experiences more when they get to do them with fellow fans.

That’s precisely why Diageo, which includes spirit brands like Guinness, Johnny Walker, Smirnoff, and Don Julio, leans into sports marketing as a brand driver.

“If you think about a game, if you think about going to a stadium, if you think about going to a bar and restaurant to watch the World Cup, our products, more often than not, are already part of that experience,” explained Rick Pineda, VP of global sports at Diageo.

The goal is for Diageo brands to complement and enhance the sports-watching experience, not hijack it. “So, as people are experiencing this amazing moment in sports, we are along for the ride and are additive, not distracting or trying to interject into a place where we don’t belong,” said Pineda.

Likewise, Screenvision Media, which runs 20-minute shows that include regional and national advertising before feature films across 14,000 screens, has been experimenting with new interactive and immersive experiences before moviegoers tuck their phones away, shared McGaw. These experiences include using mobile phones to interact with on-screen trivia, QR codes, or shopability.

“We recognize that earlier on the show, folks are on their phone,” McGaw said. “How do we give opportunities for advertisers to be a part of that process as well?”

The discussion shifted to explore the brand opportunities possible with the World Cup, as well as with award shows and big films.

Pineda shared that Diageo is the first-ever spirits partner for FIFA and they’ve run with it, creating tailored campaigns based on location. In Mexico, for example, they developed a huge campaign with Don Julio, including a limited-time Don Julio bottle that’s an homage to the World Cup trophy.

“People will pick the bottle up and showcase it, much like soccer players that win the trophy do at the end of the World Cup,” explained Pineda. “So, it’s about finding those moments of connection.”

McGaw agreed that there is power in community connection, sharing that Screenvision Media is leaning more toward in-person experiences as well, including recent watch parties for the BET Awards in three cities and product sampling with L’Oreal around The Devil Wears Prada 2 before and after the show.

“The experiential opportunity that cinema delivers for your creative—in addition to a 40-foot screen and surround sound—is very unique,” said McGaw. “It’s this opportunity for advertisers to have consumers not just experience their brand on a screen, but experience the brand in real life, in person, when they’re in an environment that is already leaning into being out more and more likely to shop before or after a movie.”

articles.title: Tracksuit Acquires AI Brand-Monitoring Startup Hall
articles.fullContent:
By Trishla Ostwal The future of brands gets decided here. Join the industry’s top marketers at Brandweek for the ideas, insights, and connections shaping what’s next. Get your ticket.

Brand tracking startup Tracksuit has acquired Hall, a Sydney based startup that measures how brands appear in responses from AI assistants, the company announced on Monday. Terms of the acquisition weren’t disclosed.

The deal adds AI-monitoring capability to Tracksuit’s existing business of surveying consumer sentiment for brands including Unilever and Pfizer.

Marketers are growing increasingly concerned about how their products are described–or omitted–by AI assistants like OpenAI’s ChatGPT and Google’s Gemini. “Brands are particularly worried about their brand being authentic for both humans and the LLMs” said Connor Archbold, Tracksuit’s CEO, further describing the resulting product as one built to show marketers “how your brand is perceived relative to and connected to what human sentiment is.”

The new product will track visibility across ChatGPT, and Google’s AI Mode–these platforms command the most users today, Archbold said.

Archbold declined to specify the purchase price or say whether the transaction was funded with cash, stock, or combination.

AI Search Will Top $100B In Ad Revenue by 2030: WPP Media Hall’s four-person team will join Tracksuit, including founder Kai Forsyth, who will become the company’s principal product manager and will lead production direction for AI-visibility, Archbold said.

Founded in 2021, Tracksuit sells a subscription dashboard that tracks brand awareness and purchase intent across nearly 20,000 brands in 25 markets. This acquisition comes on the heels of its  $25 million Series B raised in 2025, ADWEEK previously reported. Archbold said the company has no plans to raise capital in the near term.

Hall, founded in 2023, built tools to measure how brands surface across AI assistants–part of a wave of startups, including Profound and Bluefish , pitching so-called answer-engine optimization tools to marketers as search traffic shifts from Google to AI assistants.

EXCLUSIVE: YouTube Overtakes Reddit as Go-To Citation Source on AI Search Still, measuring that shift remains inexact. There’s no standard, verified methodology on how LLMs select which brands to surface in response to a given query, and vendors in this category have offered competition approaches to quantify AI visibility.

Archbold said that standardization is a function of scale. “Being able to look at brand health across 20,000 brands, and saying ‘these are the patterns that we see, and  this is what’s happening in the market,’” he said.

The AI-visibility product is expected to become available to Tracksuit’s existing customers this year, with new customers able to join a waitlist, the company said.

View the Pitch Deck Tracksuit Used to Raise $25M, Making Brand Tracking Affordable With AI Trishla Ostwal

Trishla is an Adweek staff reporter covering AI and tech.

articles.title: Marketing Vanguard at Cannes: Stop Forcing Marketing Into False Binaries ft. Jill Kramer of Mastercard
articles.fullContent:
By Jenny Rooney The future of brands gets decided here. Join the industry’s top marketers at Brandweek for the ideas, insights, and connections shaping what’s next. Get your ticket.

Marketing Vanguard continues its Cannes Lions 2026 series as host Jenny Rooney sits down with Jill Kramer, chief marketing and communications officer at Mastercard.

In this conversation, Jill reveals the false binaries marketing keeps boxing itself into: AI vs. creativity, brand vs. performance, strategy vs. execution, “what’s in” vs. what’s “dead.”

Her point is clear: the strongest marketing does not live at the extremes. It lives in the gray area where accountability, emotion, creativity, and business outcomes all have to work together.

Why AI should sharpen creative focus, not replace the human craft

How brand and performance marketing became a false choice

Why the best marketing happens in the “soft emotional middle”

How prioritization can become a creative act for a new CMO

Why leaders now need to go high and deep at the same time

How to shift from doing everything yourself to enabling the team

Why the industry needs to stop arguing in extremes and talk more about the work

This episode is part of a special vidcast series that was recorded live during Cannes Lions 2026 and presented in partnership with Edelman .

Jill Kramer is the chief marketing and communications officer at Mastercard, bringing deep experience across brand strategy, creative leadership and enterprise marketing transformation. Before joining Mastercard, she held senior leadership roles at Accenture, where she progressed from head of advertising, to brand, and finally, to chief marketing and communications officer.

Earlier, she also built her foundation on the agency side at BBDO. At Mastercard, Jill brings a perspective shaped by both sides of the table: the agency instinct for creative craft and the brand-side understanding of business complexity, accountability, and internal alignment.

Her leadership is rooted in balance: brand and performance, creativity and AI, strategic vision and hands-on involvement.

[02:30] Why AI Can Make Creativity Better — Jill pushes back on the idea that AI and creativity are in opposition. Creativity is still a human craft, but AI can help sharpen focus on the core idea, the insight and the customer understanding behind the work. The smartest use of AI is not to bypass the art. It is to clear space for the art to get stronger. For CMOs, that means using the tool where it serves the work, without pretending the tool is the work.

[04:34] Brand and Performance Are Not Opposites — Jill reflects on the industry’s pendulum swing from brand to performance and argues that the answer was never one or the other. At Mastercard, brand has always been deeply valued, but the wider marketing conversation often forces itself into extremes. Performance brought useful accountability and transparency, but overcorrecting into performance-only thinking can flatten the emotional power of marketing. The future is in integration.

[05:48] The Gray Area Is Where Real Marketing Happens — Jill calls out how strange it is that marketing, a craft built on emotion, culture and persuasion, keeps talking about itself in exacting binaries. Her point is sharper: real marketing lives in the gray area, in the soft emotional middle where data, creativity, accountability and consumer feeling all collide. That nuance is the work.

[09:24] Why Modern CMOs Need to Go High and Deep — Jill argues that marketing leadership cannot sit at a distance anymore. Technology is changing too fast, decisions are moving too quickly, and accountability is too high. The leaders who will thrive are the ones who can hold the 30,000-foot view and still get close to the work when needed. This is not micromanagement; it is business fluency. CMOs cannot lead transformation if they are waiting for someone else to explain the tools, the tradeoffs or the decisions.

[12:40] Stop Speaking in Extremes — Jill’s provocation for the industry is simple: stop talking in extremes. Stop declaring what is in, what is out, what is dead, and what is the next miracle. These polar-opposite arguments keep distracting the industry from the harder, more useful conversation. Because the work and ideas matter, and the ability to be inspired by what other teams are making matters. Marketing does not need another binary. It needs better attention.

Jenny Rooney is Chief Brand and Community Officer, leading strategy for the overall ADWEEK brand as well as the ways in which we serve and support our audiences with high-value content, products, partnerships and experiences, notably through our community programs such as Marketing Vanguard.

articles.description: <p>There’s no denying it: <a href="https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/how-search-behaviors-are-changing">Search behaviors have changed</a>. As more queries are answered directly by AI Overviews, ChatGPT, and Perplexity, the <a href="https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/seo-predict
articles.summary: <p>There’s no denying it: <a href="https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/how-search-behaviors-are-changing">Search behaviors have changed</a>. As more queries are answered directly by AI Overviews, ChatGPT, and Perplexity, the <a href="https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/seo-predictions">shifts reshaping search</a> are forcing marketers to rethink ranking and, just as crucially, learn <a href="https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/how-to-write-for-ai-search">how to write for AI search</a> rather than just for a list of blue links.</p> <p><a class="cta_button" href="https://www.hubspot.com/cs/ci/?pg=9dd5e54b-fbef-4dd0-bc44-1689feb1ea18&amp;pid=53&amp;ecid=&amp;hseid=&amp;hsic="><img class="hs-cta-img " style="height: auto !important; width: auto !important; max-width: 100% !important;border-width: 0px; /*hs-extra-styles*/; margin: 0 auto; display: block; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px" alt="Get Started with HubSpot's AEO Tool" height="58" width="335" src="https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/53/9dd5e54b-fbef-4dd0-bc44-1689feb1ea18.png" align="middle"></a></p> <p>The urgency is already showing up in the numbers: according to <strong><a href="https://offers.hubspot.com/state-of-aeo">HubSpot’s 2026 State of AEO Report</a></strong>, <strong>58%</strong> of marketers say their businesses are optimizing content for answer engines. Answer engine optimization (AEO) has moved from a fringe experiment to a mainstream priority.</p> <p>But knowing AEO matters and knowing <em>what to actually do</em> are two different things. The content most likely to be cited shares a handful of recognizable traits that primarily hinge on structure.</p> <p>In this post, I’ll break down the <a href="https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/best-content-for-sge">content formats that perform best in AI search</a>, from question-led headings and direct-answer summaries to <a href="https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/structured-data">structured data</a> and a deliberate <a href="https://blog.hubspot.com/customers/how-to-optimize-your-site-structure-for-maximum-seo-impact">site structure built for maximum impact</a>.</p> <p><strong>Table of Contents</strong></p> <ul> Headings and Direct-Answer Summaries</a></li> <li><a href="#theme-2-semantic-schema-and-entity-modeling">Theme 2: Semantic Schema and Entity Modeling</a></li> <li><a href="#theme-3-authoritative-signals-and-trust-markers">Theme 3: Authoritative Signals and Trust Markers</a></li> <li><a href="#theme-4-strategic-internal-linking-architecture">Theme 4: Strategic Internal Linking Architecture</a></li> <li><a href="#theme-5-passage-level-optimization-for-extraction">Theme 5: Passage-Level Optimization for Extraction</a></li> <li><a href="#how-to-align-structural-themes-with-googles-quality-guidelines">How to Align Structural Themes with Google’s Quality Guidelines</a></li> <li><a href="#how-to-measure-citation-performance-and-structural-impact">How to Measure Citation Performance and Structural Impact</a></li> <li><a href="#how-to-operationalize-high-citation-content-themes">How to Operationalize High-Citation Content Themes</a></li> <li><a href="#frequently-asked-questions-faqs-about-structuring-content-for-answer-engine-citations">Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) About Structuring Content for Answer Engine Citations</a></li> </ul> <a></a> <h2>Why Content Structure Drives Citation Rates in Answer Engines</h2> <p>Search is shifting from a list of links to a single, synthesized answer.</p> <p>Instead of returning a list of links, Google’s AI Overviews, ChatGPT, and Perplexity now:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Read a page</strong></li> <li><strong>Lift the passage that best answers a query</strong></li> <li><strong>Cite the source inline</strong></li> </ul> <p>These days, visibility is no longer about ranking among the top 10 blue links. Nowadays, it’s about whether an answer engine can extract a clean, self-contained passage from your page and attribute it to your brand.</p> <p>That said, page structure is <em>the</em> mechanism that enables that extraction.</p> <p>Here’s a detailed breakdown on why:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Answer engines prioritize content with clarity.</strong> Well-formed headings, short declarative passages, and explicit question-and-answer pairings give parsers obvious boundaries to pull from.</li> <li><strong>The cleaner those boundaries, the higher the citation likelihood.</strong> Poorly structured pages force the engine to guess, and ambiguous passages rarely get cited.</li> <li><strong>The takeaway for AEO:</strong> the same structural themes that make a page easy to parse correlate with higher citation frequency.</li> </ul> <h3><strong>Structural Themes That Correlate with Citations</strong></h3> <p>Clear structure and direct answers recur across cited content.

articles.title: How To Measure AI Search Visibility via @sejournal, @slobodanmanic
articles.fullContent:
AI search visibility has become the new vanity metric, and most teams are measuring the wrong number. The AI visibility tools multiplying across the market count how often a model mentions you, or cites you, when someone types a prompt. That number feels like progress because it looks like the rank tracking we have done for 20 years. It is not the same thing, and the gap between what these tools count and what actually moves your business keeps widening. This is a guide to the metrics that matter in AI search, the ones that only look like they matter, and how to tell them apart, built from six No Hacks podcast conversations with people who do this for a living and the data that landed this month.

The dominant way to measure AI search right now is prompt tracking, and as most teams use it, it is the wrong instrument. The pitch is familiar: a tool types a set of prompts into ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google’s AI Overviews, and reports how often your brand shows up. It looks like rank tracking, so it sells. I get more pitches every week from companies that built one and want to come on the podcast. There are far more of them than the market needs, and the attention is rushing toward the most visible, most obvious thing to count, whether or not it is the thing that matters.

Jono Alderson , a technical SEO consultant, put the objection this way when he came on the show. “We need to instead try and influence how the machine perceives us. And that’s not prompt tracking, which is what everyone is doing at the moment,” he said. “There is a place for that, but it’s far smaller than I think.” His diagnosis of why these dashboards feel off is sharper still: “It’s copy-paste the current modality of rank tracking into a new thing. It doesn’t really fit, but it’s better than nothing.”

Prompt tracking also rests on a guess about user behavior that mostly is not grounded. You invent a list of prompts you hope your customers type, then measure yourself against them. For most brands, that list has little to do with what real people are actually asking, and an AI prompt is not a keyword. Ground those prompts in real search data instead, and you hit a second problem: AI is corrupting that data faster than you can read it.

I have a first-hand reason. Last year, I helped investigate a strange leak: real users’ ChatGPT prompts were turning up inside Google Search Console, the report website owners use to track search traffic. I worked on it with the analytics consultant Jason Packer, who published the findings on Quantable , and Ars Technica , and a long list of outlets covered it. We traced it to a bugged prompt box that made ChatGPT search almost every time, with a ChatGPT URL leading the queries that Google then tokenized, so websites ranking for those terms saw strangers’ private prompts in their dashboards. My own concern at the time, which Ars Technica quoted, was that the leak was contributing to “crocodile mouth” in Search Console, the pattern where impressions spike while clicks dip.

The leak was the visible version of a problem that is now everywhere and impossible to see. AI systems hit Google constantly to ground their answers, fanning a single prompt out into several parallel queries and reading results no human ever lays eyes on. Every one of those searches lands as an impression on the pages that rank for it. So when your impressions climb while your clicks do not, that is not rising human demand. A growing share of it is machines searching on someone’s behalf and consuming the answer without ever clicking through. You can watch the same distortion in search-trend and keyword-volume data , where the curves climb and stop telling you how much of the demand is even human. Search Console is a noisy, leaky place to read AI behavior, and the instruments most people reach for are measuring a number that AI is busy inflating. Underneath nearly all of this is still web search, with grounding and query fan-out doing the work. Google built AI visibility reporting straight into Search Console , which tells you where it thinks this is heading, but the report shows impressions, not AI clicks. It hands you the number AI inflates and withholds the one that would let you check it.

The single most important distinction in AI search measurement is that a citation is not a recommendation. A citation is when a model names your page as a source under its answer. A recommendation is when the model tells the user to choose you. Most tools count the first and let you assume it means the second. It does not.

Lily Ray pulled the AI Overview answers for 100 business software “best of” queries at three checkpoints, in April, May, and June 2026. When a brand’s own self-promotional listicle was cited as a source, that brand was left out of the actual recommendation 69% of the time , 224 of the 323 self-promotional listicles cited. Google was reading the page and then recommending the competitors named inside it. Separately, Jeff Oxford’s team at Visibility Labs tested 20,000 ChatGPT responses and found product recommendations changed 80.2% once search was switched on, with only a weak 0.4 correlation between being cited and being recommended. BrightEdge, looking across five engines , found the same shape from another angle: Source overlap between engine pairs ranged from 16% to 59%, but the set of recommended brands stayed in a tighter 36% to 55% band. And Kevin Indig’s analysis of 3.7 million citations found that 91% of cited URLs appear in only one engine, so even your citation footprint does not travel.

Alisa Scharf , the Chief AI Officer at Seer Interactive, had been arguing the same thing for longer. “Citations are an even worse metric than page one visibility,” she told me, “because they don’t necessarily indicate that your brand is mentioned in that response. We think of it as a leading indicator, akin to being on page two or page three of Google.” She laid the levels out as a hierarchy: “There’s the citation where your webpage is mentioned. There’s the mention where you’ve got your brand in the response. But rarely is ChatGPT or Claude specifically saying, you should go with X.” That last step, the recommendation, is the one that pays, and it is the one prompt-tracking scores treat as the same thing as a footnote.

Malte Landwehr , who runs product and marketing at the AI search platform Peec AI, had the cleanest illustration of how far citation and recommendation can drift apart. He described a case where a now-defunct tool became one of the most-cited sources behind ChatGPT’s answers in its category. “They didn’t gain visibility as a brand,” he said. “But they now have power over what brands are recommended by LLMs.” Being the source and being the choice are not the same measurement.

A single measurement of an AI answer is close to worthless, because the answer is different every time you ask. This is the part prompt-tracking dashboards paper over: They show you a number as if it were stable.

Rand Fishkin , who runs the audience-research firm SparkToro, ran the study that quantified it. “You are not getting an answer when you ask,” he told me. “You are getting one of thousands or potentially millions of answers, and every time you ask, it’s gonna be different. Every different person who asks is gonna get a different list, a different number of items, a different order, and a different set of recommendations.” How different is that? “In order to get two lists of brands that are the same in an answer, on average, you would need to ask Claude or ChatGPT 1,500 times before you get two answers with the same list of brands in the same order.”

That number is the whole case against single-shot measurement. It does not mean AI visibility is unmeasurable. It means it has to be measured the way you measure a poll, not the way you check a rank. He was explicit that the signal is there if you do the work: “If you ask the right number of prompts, the right number of times, with some variability, you can get a statistical number that’s basically plus or minus 5%, or plus or minus 1% if you go really hard.” The instrument is fine. The problem is that most tools run it once and call the result a ranking.

What To Measure Instead: Presence And Recommendation Share

The number that replaces prompt tracking is presence: how often you are named across the answer space, read against whether that presence turns into a recommendation and an action.

Rand Fishkin called it the only honest version of the number. “Percent of visibility is the number that’s real, that an AI tracking tool should be giving you,” he said. “It’s not like Google rank tracking. It’s more like when brands in the 20th century used to survey consumers and they would say, have you heard of Nike shoes, have you heard of Adidas shoes.” Wil Reynolds , the founder of Seer Interactive, added the instrumentation detail that almost no one tracks: not only whether you appear, but the composition of the answer over time. “If you’re tracking visibility and you don’t also track things like the number of words or brands mentioned per model per prompt over time,” he said, “you would not know that back in November ChatGPT doubled the length of the answer.” When the answer doubles, your raw visibility can rise while nothing real changed. You did not get more valuable. The same person is seeing more words.

There is a harder caveat under all of this, and it is the one he was most blunt about: visibility only counts if it is tied to the outcome . “You can be visible. That’s great,” he said. “But somebody’s gotta actually take an action for you to make any money from that visibility. If you don’t track those two metrics against each other, you’re the sucker. ”

I run these measurements on my own brand, and the result is the clearest proof I have that the work moves the metric. Right now, Google’s AI Overviews recommend No Hacks as the best podcast for AI web strategy. A month ago, they did not. I did not get there by climbing a prompt-tracking dashboard. I got there by changing what the systems know about my entity, which is a different exercise entirely. And the honest scoreboard has to account for which prompts you are even measuring. With Search Console, you have a rough, grounded idea of the queries you rank for. With prompt tracking you start with prompts you made up and put in a list, which is not grounded in what is actually happening for most brands. Measure recommendation share if you can, but know that its quality depends entirely on whether the prompts behind it are real.

The Same Vanity Metric We Already Lived Through

It took the search industry the better part of two decades to accept that impressions and clicks were, on their own, vanity numbers, because in the end, they did not have to mean revenue. We have been here before. AI visibility for the sake of visibility is the same trap wearing new clothes. It might be branding. It is not the metric that matters, and it is a very good vanity metric precisely because it is easy to make go up.

Wil Reynolds, whose episode was titled around this exact point, traced the loop directly. “The vanity metric early was rankings,” he said, “and then people went, wait, I gotta get traffic from those rankings, and then I need that traffic to turn into a business. So to me it’s just a regurgitation of what we did years ago.” Jono Alderson went further and argued the precise attribution we comforted ourselves with was never real to begin with: “the crutch and the lies that we’ve told ourselves for the last decade, that we can neatly attribute impression share through to clicks, through to actions, through to revenue. It’s never been true, and it’s getting less true.” The job, restated, is older than any of the tooling. As he put it, two decades ago, we could have decided our work was to influence how people perceive the brand.

The metric to build first is brand accuracy: whether the AI describes your entity correctly at all. Recommendation share comes after. If the model holds wrong facts about you, every downstream number is built on sand, because it is recommending, or refusing to recommend, a version of you that is not real.

This is where clarity starts. It means brand consistency across every platform, other people describing you the same way, and a coherent answer to the basic questions of who you are and what you do. Duane Forrester , who helped launch Schema.org and built Bing Webmaster Tools, framed the goal as being the trusted source rather than the high ranker . “Your goal should be to be seen as the canonical for whatever your question is,” he said. “Not rankings, but that you are the source of knowledge.” His read on why this compounds was that the machine is lazy in a useful way: “It costs money and cycles and tokens to go build trust. So if I’ve done all that work and I trust you, and you’re a good answer, and my consumer is happy with that answer, why would I change?”

Alisa Scharf turned this into a measurement you can actually run, which she calls a brand accuracy audit. “You come up with a list of objective criteria,” she said. “It can’t be, we want to rank for best X for Y. It’s got to be: when were you founded, where are you based, what do you sell, who do you compete against. And you take that list of queries and see, for each model, what is it consistently getting right, what is it consistently getting wrong.” That is the audit. Run your own non-negotiable facts through each engine on a schedule, and score the model on accuracy, not on whether it flattered you with a mention.

Two Blind Spots: Training Cutoff And Platform Data

Honest measurement has to name its blind spots, and AI search has two big ones. The first is the training-data cutoff. A meaningful share of answers comes from what the model already learned before any live grounding kicks in, frozen at a date you do not control, and we do not yet have a clean way to measure whether the work we do today is moving those baked-in answers at all. You can be optimizing hard against a version of the model’s knowledge that is months stale.

The second is platform data, and here the shape of the market matters. The frontier model companies have little reason to hand you usage data. There is no obvious world where OpenAI or Anthropic exposes how its model decided what to recommend. The companies that might give you something are the ones with a larger ecosystem to protect: Google is adding AI impressions to Search Console, and Microsoft surfaces a version of this through Bing Webmaster Tools, because they run both the model and a measurement surface that benefits from your attention. It is weak data, and it is something rather than nothing. Whether the pure-play model companies ever open this up is a genuine open question, and a lot of how measurable AI search becomes depends on the answer.

Do you know who you are, and what you want people to think about you? Are you communicating that clearly, and in enough different places, that AI systems can form an accurate picture of your entity rather than a guess? Be clear and be consistent. It sounds almost too simple to write down, and yet schema, the pages on your website, every social profile, and every place you are mentioned all have to say the same unambiguous thing about what you are, what your company is called, and who is behind it.

There is a reason this is becoming the deterministic core of the work, not the soft branding edge of it. A German court recently held Google liable for false statements its AI Overview generated about a business , on the reasoning that the AI answer is Google’s own speech. Here is a thesis I cannot prove yet, and I want to be clear it is speculation: A platform that is now legally on the hook for what its AI says about you has a strong incentive to surface only the entities it is confident about. Picture a confidence threshold, some internal certainty score, where if the system is sure enough about who you are, it includes you, and if it is not, it leaves you out rather than risk being wrong. The exact number is invented. The direction feels right. And if it is right, then the most important thing you can measure is not how often you appear. It is how certain the machine is that it knows you, because that certainty is about to decide whether you appear at all.

Stop Treating AI Visibility As One Problem. It’s Actually Three, On Three Different Layers

articles.title: Ubersuggest MCP Connector: Use 37 SEO Tools Inside Claude, Cursor and More
articles.fullContent:
You already use an AI assistant to write, think, and research. But every time you need SEO data (keyword volumes, competitor rankings, backlink counts), you stop. You open Ubersuggest. You pull the report. You copy the numbers. You switch back to the AI. You paste.

That workflow just became obsolete.

The Ubersuggest MCP connector integrates over 37 SEO tools directly into any MCP-compatible AI assistant: Claude, Cursor, Windsurf, and more. You ask your AI assistant a question. It queries Ubersuggest in real time. You get an answer.

No dashboards. No tab-switching. No copy-paste. Just your AI assistant, with Ubersuggest’s full keyword database and domain analysis engine running underneath it.

This post explains what MCP is, what’s inside the Ubersuggest connector, and how to set it up in about two minutes.

What Is MCP? (The 60-Second Version)

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard, originally developed by Anthropic and now widely adopted, that lets AI assistants connect directly to external tools and data sources. Think of it as a universal plugin system for AI: instead of an AI assistant working only with what’s already in its training data, MCP lets it query live, real-world information on demand.

When you connect the Ubersuggest MCP to your AI assistant, that assistant can now call Ubersuggest’s APIs directly during your conversation. You don’t have to do anything differently. You just ask questions, and your AI assistant fetches the data.

The protocol uses OAuth 2.0 authentication, so your Ubersuggest account credentials are secured. The connection is read-only. No changes are made to your account through the MCP.

What the Ubersuggest MCP Gives You: 37 Tools Across 7 Categories

The connector gives your AI assistant access to the full Ubersuggest toolset, organized across seven categories:

1. Domain Analysis (8 tools)

Pull domain authority scores, traffic estimates, top-performing pages, organic keyword counts, and year-over-year performance trends for any domain, yours or a competitor’s.

2. Keyword Research (7 tools)

Access search volume, keyword difficulty, CPC, trend data, and keyword suggestions for any query in over 100 million keywords. Filter by country, language, or intent type.

3. Backlinks (5 tools)

Query backlink profiles: total backlinks, referring domains, domain rating of linking sites, anchor text distribution, and new/lost link history.

4. Site Audit (4 tools)

Run and retrieve site audit data: broken links, crawl errors, page speed issues, missing meta tags, and technical health scores.

5. Content (2 tools)

Get content ideas, SEO content scores, and optimization recommendations for target keywords.

6. Projects (7 tools)

Access your Ubersuggest project data: rank tracking results, keyword position history, and your tracked competitor comparisons.

7. Utilities (4 tools)

Helper functions for SERP data, related keyword expansion, and search intent classification.

Why This Changes Your SEO Workflow

The real value of the Ubersuggest MCP is the elimination of context-switching.

Here’s what a typical SEO research session looks like today: open AI assistant, form a hypothesis, open Ubersuggest, pull keyword data, copy to clipboard, paste into AI, continue the analysis, realize you need competitor data, open Ubersuggest again, pull domain data, copy, paste, repeat.

With the MCP connector, that session becomes: open AI assistant, form a hypothesis, ask for the data. The AI handles the rest.

This is especially powerful for:

  • Agency workflows. Client reporting that used to require pulling 5 reports from 3 tools now happens inside a single AI conversation. Ask for the data, the interpretation, and a summary, all in sequence, in one thread.
  • Competitive research. Ask your AI assistant to compare multiple domains side-by-side, pull backlink profiles, and identify keyword gaps, all in real time.
  • Content strategy. Generate a content plan, immediately check volume and difficulty for each topic, and revise based on real data, without leaving the conversation.
  • Rank monitoring. Ask your AI to review last week’s ranking changes, flag drops, and draft a prioritized action plan. Done in one conversation instead of a multi-tab reporting session.

articles.fullContent:
Key takeaways

  1. The most versatile Facebook ad size is 1080 x 1080 pixels (1:1), which works across Feed, Marketplace, carousel, and search results placements.
  2. For Stories and Reels, use 1080 x 1920 pixels (9:16) to fill the full screen on mobile.
  3. When running Feed ads on mobile, 4:5 aspect ratio (1080 x 1350) gives you more screen real estate than 1:1.

Facebook ad sizes at a glance

We cover each ad format in more detail below, but here is a quick overview of all popular facebook ad sizes and specs at a glance.

Facebook advertising guidelines

Before you dive into placement-specific sizes, it helps to know the rules that apply to almost every Facebook ad you’ll run. A few quick notes first: “facebook ad sizes” and “meta ad sizes” refer to the exact same specs, since Facebook is one product inside Meta’s family of apps. For a detailed overview of the different formats, check out this guide on Facebook ad tools.

Here are the universal guidelines to keep in mind:

  • Default to 1080 x 1080 pixels. If you can only make one asset, the 1:1 square is the safest choice because it displays cleanly across the most placements.
  • Use 4:5 for mobile Feed. A 4:5 aspect ratio (1080 x 1350) takes up more vertical space in the mobile feed, so your ad occupies more of the screen as people scroll.
  • Go 9:16 for full-screen placements. Stories and Reels are vertical, so use 1080 x 1920 pixels to fill the whole screen.
  • Keep text on images light. Meta no longer enforces the old 20% text rule, but ads with less text on the image still tend to get broader delivery and better results, so put your key message in the ad caption instead.
  • Respect safe zones. On vertical formats, UI elements and CTA buttons cover the top and bottom of the frame. Keep your key visuals and copy in the center of the canvas.
  • Give Advantage+ room to work. When you use Advantage+ placements through your ads manager, Meta automatically adjusts and crops your creative to fit each placement, so upload the highest-resolution assets you can.

Bonus: Get the Facebook advertising cheat sheet for 2022. The free resource includes key audience insights, recommended ad types, and tips for success.

Facebook feed ad sizes

The Facebook news feed is probably what you think of most when it comes to Facebook ad sizes. It’s the endlessly scrollable place to find all your friends’ status updates, photo posts, videos, and more, plus content from brands and Facebook Pages you follow.

Facebook feed ads come in many styles, from carousel ads to single image or video ads, and choosing the right format is a key part of your ad campaign strategy. The second item in a user’s feed is an ad, with more mixed between organic content as a user continues scrolling.

For the mobile feed, 4:5 aspect ratio is usually the better choice because it takes up more screen space and pushes competing content out of view, a difference you can confirm in your paid ad analytics, especially since 81.8% of Facebook users access it only via mobile.

If your ad will run across multiple placements, 1:1 is the safer default since it crops predictably everywhere. The same feed specs apply if you’re advertising in the dedicated video feed (the video tab you reach from the desktop app or the mobile menu), where video ads appear with a “sponsored” label alongside organic clips.
